item.content:encoded:
frightening increase in the effectiveness of the human immune system.

This is far from clinical tone.

Also, there isn't anything interesting here. There's no story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s the Fountain of Youth. The O-5’s use it to stay young, and tell everyone else it’s toxic and kill them on-spot if they come in contact with it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 4.0
Title: Ndongo Stream.jpg
Author: Yona Tientcheu
Release year: 2019

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote for now.

This page has been around for 11 years, and is very unlikely to change in the near future. I'd recommend just voting how you feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote for now.

I think that this article could be much improved with a side-story or side-article depicting what an average foundation researcher would see if they attempted to look up SCP-006.

Perhaps something suggesting that the liquid initially improves immune function and repairs damage due to aging, but then causes the immune system to go haywire in a way that is extremely painful for the subject.

It's apparent why O5s would want lesser employees to be unaware of the fact that this liquid (basically) grants immortality, but more detail as to what the cover story actually is could be a nice improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was unsure of my stance towards this article but then I noticed the lack of censorship, even when providing information such as a year or the location. This is quite a rare thing to see in the early articles and it's actually kind of cathartic. That being said, initially I thought of the article as being to bare bones but I don't really know what else you could add to it. It gets a +1 from me but only by a hair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Termination seems a bit over the top for just coming in contact with the liquid from the spring. At least explain why termination would be the only viable option, perhaps in an incident report or similar.

Also, such an SCP would be extremely useful in regards to researching other, more harmful SCPs. Gonna have to -1 this one for now I'm afraid.

item.title: Re-write! Re-write! Re-write!
item.content:encoded: Too short for something that's supposed to be so huge in the eyes of the O5. -1, needs a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this SCP is essentially the fountain of youth, but a spring instead? Either way,
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The liquid emitted from the spring has been chemically identified as simple mineral water in 1902, but has the unusual property of "health".

Our new medicine could help you spring into action.

….. Im sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You make a good point actually that I didn't consider before. Despite the seemingly poorly written nature of this article it does in fact convey more than enough in context clues to allow the reader to figure out what is being referred to. I would argue though that seeing as it's the Fountain of Youth we're talking about here, it doesn't take much to convey that to the reader.

Also, this is the Foundation we're talking about so super secret, even to the rest of the Foundation, or not you can bet that they're going to treat it just as clinically as any other skip which is where this really falls short. It reads more like hastily written field notes than a proper SCP report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if someone were to give this to 49. Would it help him cure the pestilence? Would it do nothing? Would it make things worse? I think it'd be pretty interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting on the basis that this manages to tell its story using only context clues. I know other articles have done this as well, but I like how this was done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course, the idea of The Fountain of Youth is pretty awesome. But, it would be good to have a few more details on this one. It's strange that, "All personnel interacting with SCP-006 in any physical way are required to wear modified Class VI BNC suits", because they would have to ingest the liquid. Unless the suggestion is that mere contact with the liquid transfers some sort of anomalous affect. However, this is not stated. So, all of the extra procedures of submersing personnel to test their suits seems pointless. A simple loosely enclosed helmet would prevent ingestion.

Also, while the most efficient termination method is incineration, this seems to contradict the properties of, "heightened excitement of cellular duplication, vastly improved abilities in the repair of damaged tissue". One would imagine that after ingestion of the liquid (again not just contact with it), an average humanoid would be able to withstand heat and fire better than a humanoid that did not ingest the liquid. So, would burning at the stake even work? Would unapproved individuals affected by the liquid need to be terminated in a special blast furnace?

Maybe the lack of clarification is part of the point of this entry, as it does get you thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know. I just wish it was executed better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
006, water that heals the sick.
Eeeeehhhh.

I think the twist was that it's meant to be the fountain of youth (hence the title) and only the O5s can use it or something. Been awhile since I read this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
006, water that heals the sick.
Eeeeehhhh.
It’s old.
It doesn’t feel too bad, but it’s not good.
This is one of those where everything that could be said, has been said. I agree with everyone else here, this one could use a few touches.
I like the underlying concept a lot, but it’s unclear and muddy, and it’s confusing, and it’s asking for more material, such as the fake reports.
I don’t feel as if this is the worst old scip, but I have a lot if the same problems with it as the others. Overall, it’s a resounding eh.
I do hope one of the fake reports actually gives us a reason for the incineration too.
+0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do think the incosistency of the spring's abilities across different species makes little sense to write about without a least hinting towards a reason.

I'm also really wonderimg whether consuming the water produces its benefits permanently, or only for a time. It's never stated one way or the other, and the first time I read this article I assumed the latter, while others have evidently assumed the former.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with a lot of the recent comments on this skip. It just feels "off" in a number of ways and doesn't really feel consistent within the generally established methodology of skips overall.

Structure/Format - This only loosely fits within the general "Research Report" format. Little things like the clearance bit at the top which in most other cases I've seen take the form of expandos or similar to simulate actually getting access.

Discovery & Containment - Very little description of the containment procedures or facilities are given. They almost seem like an afterthought here and not one of the more important bits they are in other skips. As well, discovery of the anomaly is given only a passing mention.

Ambiguity - The whole skip leaves much to the imagination and not in the good way that most skips do so well but more in a "What does this even mean? Why though?" etc type way. The comment by CaioXG002 is a good breakdown of what I'm talking about. The way that the incineration bit is worded implies that there's good reason this should be the termination method but the rest of the skip after that would seem to imply that the only reason this would be done is to prevent discovery and not because of anything dangerous otherwise. If that's true though then why incineration? The Foundation would I'm sure be able to fully conceal and cover-up a termination without needing to incinerate.

All in all I thought it was an ok skip and a decent Fountain of Youth homage but it has a lot of unrealized potential that is stymied by inconsistencies and non-useful vagaries. I was actually surprised to see it had a high rating considering the standards I've seen on the site thus far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to the nature of SCP-006, the most effective termination method is incineration.

Given the nature of the rest of the article, I'm actually wondering if this bit is supposed to sound genuine, like, is literally burning someone the only way to guarantee they will die after using the fountain of youth or is this supposed to be the O5 being needlessly cruel just to show how much they went insane with power because of SCP-006? Like, I get the implication that shooting someone who was granted regenerative powers will often fail to kill the person, so that's not enough, but I find hard to believe that even beheading them (less cruel than incineration) would fail to guarantee they would die, like, I don't think drinking from SCP-006 can make your body regenerate a head and/or your head regenerate a body. Again, I don't think this doesn't fit the article, the O5 burning to death someone who discovered their fountain of youth fits regardless of interpretation, just wondering if the implication is what I thought or if the implication was played straight after all.

item.title: SCP006-TerO5
item.content:encoded: Will SCP006-TerO5 ever be posted?

item.title: Re: A flawed SCP
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to see some changes as well. Ideally a write-up describing the spring's water as mollecularly indistiguishable from normal water, but with lethal effects (similar to SCP-009) that necessitate careful handling and occassional delivery of samples to a classified site.

This would be followed by an Overseer security-restricted addendum, similar the the write-up of SCP-008, indicating that the above write-up is a fabrication to hide the true nature of SCP-006, and giving the actual details.

item.title: A flawed SCP
item.content:encoded:
I am really into Series I, and I think they are actually better than more recent series. But this SCP is simply not well explained, and it makes no sense. It sounds like an overly elaborated SCP 500 and I'm surprised this one has that many upvotes, specially because it is so short.

Overall, I think this is due to a rewrite. I'll try contacting the writer. If all else fails, I'll try getting the Staff involved. It's not fair one of the earliest SCPs is not up to the standards and, just because it's old, it doesn't means it shouldn't be improved.

My plans for a rewrite would be some format screw. After all, this is a SCP that only Overseers know, and what keeps them alive and well for so long. It's a big secret that deserves to look like one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To rewrite a page, you have to get permission from the author of this page, or, in case they are not available, from the staff. You can read about rewrites here. But since this article is way old and has a high rating, I doubt that your suggestion will be approved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have an idea to expand on this. What if we made three iterations of this. It was stated that they exist. It was stated that the O5's give one of two false iterations of this document to people. I know it wasn't around, but SCP-3133 is the perfect example of this. Could we change this article to make it look like that?

item.title: Re: Doesn't strike me with the same feeling as other oldies
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation's goal is to protect humanity be maintaining a baseline reality. Allowing something like this for the general public and humanity to use would, in the eyes of the O5 council, be a violation of this principle of protecting the baseline reality.

Although I do agree with you that it just isn't very good, but that's because it's just the fountain of youth and nothing more.

item.title: Doesn't strike me with the same feeling as other oldies
item.content:encoded: This is proboraly the only old one that I don't like. I know not all things are supposed to be "edgy/gonna kill you in your sleep" but it's just not as good. Plus one of the foundation's goals is to help humanity, I mean they're trying to do it with 999 and the thaumiels, but still. -1 for me, but if there was a good re write then I would totally upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damm, the first SCP i ever read. This takes me way back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Strongly agreeing with this. It may not be a good article, but it's one that sticks out in my memory with the picture.

The first author is clearly long gone. Doesn't staff have purview?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you think exposing SCP-682 to this would help or hurt the destruction process?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't really like this article either way, but it would still be better with a picture. Some googling found me these:

  • This, which is pretty similar to the original, and is public domain.
  • This, which is even more of a "very small spring", and IMO just looks better, but also has more surrounding flora which may or may not be appropriate for Astrakhan. It's under CC BY-SA 2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP, and I want to upvote, but I just can't bring myself to. It's just to jankily worded, and nothing quite 'fits' in the article. I'd like it if a staff member could rewrite this, but I suspect we've got another 173 situation here.

I find this to be quite depressing, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So far this is the first SCP in the series I honestly can't give a good rating for. While most other short SCPs seem capable of establishing a great deal within their small frame, this one is just too confusing for me to actually enjoy it. The prior revisions in reading definitely assist it to some extent but still don't impact the overall idea. It'd be a bit more interesting if there were a specific background to the secrecy of the SCP as well

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'SCPs you wish were real'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As TimetravellingGuy has mentioned, maybe this is due for a rewrite. If anyone wants to, anyways, because I don't really care enough to do anthing except downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but I feel like more could have been added to this. It just feels like its been half-done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One or several images in this article have been removed due to non-compliance with the Creative Commons license. They may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.


This automated message was sent on behalf of anqxyr. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact anqxyr in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was deliverred incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been re-reading older articles since I got accepted here and this one fails to hold a candle to what I feel is the site's current standards.

 unusual property of "health".

I feel like this line could be tweaked to "unusual property of restoring health" to better describe what it actually does. Though this is just me being pedantic.

Also, I agree with what the most recent comments are saying about this article. There are loads of interesting ways to present this one like experiment gone wrong, water polluted by an anomaly, or maybe even make it so when a person offers/gives the water to another person, their health is drained/transferred to that person. I currently lack the writing skills to make this one more interesting but I'd love to see a more exciting take on this fountain of youth.

Down voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doing my part to hopefully get an old classic rewritten into a beauty. Seriously, it's the Fountain of Youth. We've literally done DEER from Outerspace! We can remake this into something interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking the same thing as linkskywalker14 back there in 2015 - this could be more interesting if we first saw the generic/vague 'killer pool' version of the article first, before making a nice twist from revealing the true nature of this item. As is, it's not too interesting.

To be honest, I do also have a problem with the core concept really - portraying the O5s as inherently corrupt seems kinda ridiculous. I would've said make this something much worse than they're portraying it as, but I feel like we've already got enough of those for now. In any case, it just doesn't sit right with me.

Down-voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doing it to an oldie makes me feel bad, but -1. It's a great concept, but it's so boringly written. The fact that the containment procedures consume quite a bit of the page doesn't help either. (I'm on mobile right now; I'll update this if the problem isn't as bad on PC.) That bold title is very awkward, too.
EDIT: Yeah, problem is just as bad on PC. I'm pretty sure it's a bad thing for the containment procedures to make up the majority of the article.

A rewrite of this would be so nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obviously SCP #6 isn't going to be up to the site's current standards in the far flung year of 2015, but I feel compelled to comment anyway.

All the elements are here for this to be a great article in the style of SCP-1000, but it lacks the presentation to make it interesting.

I'd love a fake article about a deadly poisonous pool of water that you have to be careful about touching. Perhaps the article indicates that the water needs to be periodically siphoned off for some reason, and securely shipped to Location X. Then there'd be an O5-Eyes-Only addendum describing the true nature of the SCP, the fact that it's the fountain of youth, and that each member of the O5 council should drop by Location X once a month to restore themselves.

item.title: Re: Possible Sentence Restructure
item.content:encoded: Noting that staff consensus was established for this. :>

item.title: Re: Possible Sentence Restructure
item.content:encoded: Thanks for posting to bring it to attention. This is now fixed! It definitely bothered me too.

item.title: Re: Possible Sentence Restructure
item.content:encoded: Old post, but yes, this. I know it's basically a classic entry or whatever, but that's one line I really don't like because of it not really making sense. The rest I can chalk up to it being an older article and deal with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is an scp purposed to show the corrupt leadership of the foundation. That's a cool spin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've missed the whole point of what's going on here. The O5s are using this SCP to prolong their life. And they do not want to share.

Anyone who comes into contact with the stream could understand its properties, and might spill the beans. So the O5s flat out execute anyone who might discover its properties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I feel the whole "kill anyone who touches it!!!" thing to be a little to harsh.
As the stream apparently has no psychological side effects, it seems to me like it would be ideal to use on veteran agents tasked with capture/recapture of dangerous Euclid to Keter class Spcs, but that's just how I would spin it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it doesn't need to be creepy. It just needs to be entertaining. Like, in the way it was intended, as opposed to unintentionally hilarious or whatever. And don't feel bad to downvote oldies; sometimes they get rewritten, like SCP-715.

Unfortunately, this one would get ARC'd instead of totally deleted because it's been referenced elsewhere. One can still hope for a rewrite, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to do this to an oldie, but -1. It's too straightly-played, and doesn't suck me in effectively. I'm not even sure that the basic concept could work for me as an SCP. Sure, this would need to be contained, but it's not really creepy to me.

Every idea that flows through my head as to how to make this grab and hold feels like a shoehorn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"but has the unusual property of "health"."

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this as a worldbuilding tool, but the article is boring to me. I wish there was more here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
started skipping

Snicker.

(So this post doesn't get deleted/I do actually want to comment, I agree with pretty much everything here. It's quite a doable concept, and it has admirably stood the test of time, but it could do with a loving rewrite.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first time I've reread this since I joined the site/started skipping, and this is so meh. Like, ok, the O5 Council is immortal. Ok. It makes sense in the context of this site. But the execution is just… meh. Seriously. Needs beefing up. I kind of hope this view is shared by a sufficient number of people that this can get rewritten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was written long before Thamuiel became popular and/or officia . Under the standards at the time, it was clasdified appropriately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this tagged as "self-repairing"? Also, and this is more of a headcanon-issue, why isn't it classified as Thaumiel? Is it because the council uses it unofficially?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realize this is an old post, but nail, head. This is played too straight, and way too lolFoundation. I feel like this one should go in the heritage bin with 173 — it's an important part of site history, but not TEH BEST THING EVAR!!

item.title: Possible Sentence Restructure
item.content:encoded:
"Due to political reasons, Foundation Command was aware of its existence since the 19th century, but unable to secure it until 1991."

The phrasing of this sentence makes it sound like the Foundation was aware of its existence because of the political reasons. Possibly a better alternative would be
"Foundation Command was aware of its existence since the 19th century, but unable to securite it until 1991 due to political reasons.

item.title: Re: Clarification please!
item.content:encoded:
I, on the other hand, feel the same as if I were delivered an intriguingly wrapped present and when I opened it, there was nothing inside.

It's more like a decorative bow around your package: it looks nice, but doesn't serve a real purpose other than to make it more attractive to you. If the package itself is empty, that's a problem. Translation: if your enjoyment of the article depends on fringe stuff like references to documentation that isn't further specified, the article is just not that good.
